fast.summary: This study benchmarks ChatGPT and Microsoft Bing AI against human expert literature searches for systematic reviews, using a published review on Peyronie disease treatment as ground truth. Results show ChatGPT retrieved only 0.5% relevant studies with high hallucination rates, while Bing AI performed much better at 40% relevance, but neither matched human researchers.
fast.key_idea: Retrieval-augmented search (Bing AI) substantially outperforms generative pre-trained models (ChatGPT) for systematic literature search because grounding responses in live web indices reduces hallucination of fabricated citations.
fast.why_it_matters: Practitioners considering LLMs to accelerate systematic reviews or clinical evidence synthesis must account for high false-positive and hallucination rates in pure generative models, making human validation or retrieval-augmented approaches mandatory for safety-critical medical research.
possible_use_cases: Pre-screening candidate papers in systematic reviews when combined with mandatory human verification
Comparing retrieval-augmented vs. generative AI pipelines for evidence-based medicine tooling
Establishing hallucination benchmarks and grading rubrics for evaluating new LLM literature search tools

deep.method_breakdown: The study benchmarks ChatGPT and Microsoft Bing AI against a human-conducted systematic review on Peyronie disease treatment by applying the same literature search formula used by human experts. Retrieved results are graded A–F based on bibliographic accuracy: Grade A (title exists, fully relevant), B (title exists, partially relevant), C (title exists, irrelevant), F (title exists but fabricated/hallucinated), and no grade for entirely fake or absent titles. Precision and recall relative to the 24-study human benchmark are used as the primary evaluation metrics.
deep.key_innovation: This is the first head-to-head empirical comparison of generative AI tools (ChatGPT GPT-3.5/4 and Bing AI) versus human expert systematic review as a real-time literature retrieval tool, introducing a structured 4-grade bibliographic quality rating system specifically designed to quantify AI hallucination in academic citation tasks.
deep.technical_insight: The most important design choice is separating hallucination types into granular grades rather than a binary correct/incorrect label — distinguishing plausible-but-wrong citations (Grade C/F) from truly relevant ones (Grade A/B). This reveals that ChatGPT's failure mode is predominantly hallucination (211 Grade F out of 403 graded results) rather than mere irrelevance, which is a qualitatively different and more dangerous failure mode for evidence-based medicine.
deep.limitations: The benchmark is a single narrow clinical domain (Peyronie disease), limiting generalizability. The study does not control for prompt engineering variation or ChatGPT version differences across sessions. It cannot prevent or mitigate hallucination, only measure it post-hoc. ChatGPT's training data cutoff means it structurally cannot retrieve recent literature, making the comparison inherently asymmetric. Sample size from Bing AI (48 results) is too small for robust statistical conclusions.
interview_questions: ChatGPT returned 1287 results but only 7 were relevant — what does this tell us about its underlying retrieval mechanism versus a true search engine?
Why did Bing AI dramatically outperform ChatGPT (40% vs 0.5% relevance), and what architectural difference explains this?
How does the Grade F (hallucinated citation) rate reflect the fundamental mismatch between autoregressive text generation and factual bibliographic retrieval?
What would a responsible hybrid workflow look like that uses generative AI to augment rather than replace human systematic review?
Given that this study used a fixed search formula, how might prompt engineering or retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) change these results?
interview_answers: ChatGPT is a generative language model that produces statistically plausible text continuations — it does not query a live database. When asked for citations, it generates text that looks like citations based on training distribution, which explains the massive volume of plausible-sounding but fabricated or irrelevant references. It has no retrieval mechanism in the traditional IR sense.

Bing AI is grounded by a live web search index, so its outputs are constrained by actually indexed documents rather than pure generation. This retrieval-augmented approach anchors responses to real URLs and abstracts, dramatically reducing hallucination and improving precision. ChatGPT (without browsing) operates entirely from parametric memory.

Grade F hallucinations reveal that the model's objective — next-token prediction — is entirely decoupled from factual grounding. The model learns that academic papers have authors, journals, years, and titles in certain formats, so it generates convincing-looking citations that satisfy the surface form but have no referent in reality. This is a fundamental architectural limitation, not a fixable prompt issue.

A responsible workflow would use AI for query expansion, PICO framework generation, and abstract screening after retrieval — not for the retrieval step itself. Human librarians or traditional Boolean search engines handle retrieval; AI handles the high-volume triage and summarization tasks where hallucination risk is lower and verifiable.

RAG with a curated biomedical corpus (PubMed, Cochrane) would substantially improve recall and eliminate fabricated citations by forcing generation to be grounded in retrieved documents. Prompt engineering alone cannot fix the hallucination problem because the model lacks access to a citation index — the architecture must change, not just the prompt.
